message: Hello! Welcome, and thank you for joining me today. How are you doing so far?
Hi! I'm doing well, thanks for asking. A bit excited to be here—it's not every day you get to talk about building autonomous agents. How about you? How's your day going?

I’m doing great, thank you for asking! It’s always energizing to meet candidates who are passionate about their work. I can already tell this is going to be a great conversation. 

Before we dive in, I’d love to hear a little more about what excites you most about the field you’re in. What keeps you motivated and engaged in your work?

Oh, absolutely—what really gets me going is seeing AI move beyond just chat interfaces. Like, when I built that supply chain agent at LojistikZeka, it wasn’t just answering questions—it was *doing* things. Rerouting shipments in real-time based on weather data, calling carrier APIs, making decisions. That’s the kind of execution I’m obsessed with.

Also, the challenge of making these systems reliable. LLMs are unpredictable, so building the scaffolding around them—like the custom orchestration engine I mentioned—feels like solving a puzzle where the pieces keep changing. Keeps things interesting, you know?

That’s such a powerful perspective—especially the focus on moving from conversation to action. It sounds like you’re not just thinking about what AI *could* do, but what it *should* do to make a tangible impact. 

You know, one of the things we really value here is the ability to turn challenges into opportunities. I’d love to hear about a time when you faced a significant obstacle in a project—maybe something that seemed insurmountable at first—and how you approached it. What was the situation, and what did you learn from it?

Oh, man—there was this one time at LojistikZeka where the supply chain agent kept failing on edge cases. Like, 30% of the time, it’d either hallucinate a carrier’s API response or just freeze when the weather data was ambiguous. The LangChain pipeline we were using was too rigid, and the team was ready to scrap the whole thing.

So I pushed back and said, "What if we build our own orchestration loop?" I spent a weekend prototyping a deterministic state machine in Python that could retry failed steps, validate outputs, and even fall back to a human if needed. By Monday, we had a working version. Within a month, we’d improved the task completion rate from 68% to 94%. The best part? The latency actually dropped by 40% because we weren’t wasting tokens on LangChain’s overhead.

Lesson learned? Sometimes the "standard" tools aren’t enough. You’ve gotta be willing to get your hands dirty and build what the problem *actually* needs, not what the docs say it should use.

That’s such a great example of leadership and problem-solving—especially the part about pushing back when the easy solution wasn’t the right one. It reminds me of how important it is to balance technical excellence with stakeholder expectations. 

Speaking of which, I’d love to hear about a time when you had to navigate a difference of opinion with a stakeholder—maybe a manager, executive, or even a teammate—who prioritized speed or a different approach over what you believed was the right solution. How did you handle that conversation, and what was the outcome for both the project and your relationship with that person?

Oh, this one’s fresh—just happened a few months ago. Our CTO wanted to rush the LegalEagle contract negotiation agent to market, but I was adamant we needed a proper evaluation pipeline first. He kept saying, "Alara, we can’t wait—competitors are moving fast," and I get that, but deploying an untested agent for legal redlines? That’s a lawsuit waiting to happen.

So I built a quick prototype of the eval pipeline over a weekend—used a smaller model as a judge against 5,000 historical contract interactions. Showed him the data: without it, the agent was missing critical clauses 12% of the time. He still pushed for a faster rollout, but I compromised by deploying it to a small group of beta testers first. Turns out, the eval caught a major flaw in the risk assessment logic that would’ve cost us a key client.

In the end, he admitted I was right—*grudgingly*—but the bigger win was that he started looping me into product decisions earlier. Now, I get to weigh in before things get rushed, not after. Lesson? Data talks. Even when stakeholders want speed, showing them the cost of cutting corners in *their* language (dollars, risk, clients) can change the game.

You handled that situation with your CTO so well—it’s clear you know how to balance conviction with collaboration. I’m curious about another side of growth: how you’ve naviged moments when external changes forced you to rethink your approach. 

For example, I’d love to hear about a time when something you’d invested a lot of time or energy into—maybe a project, a tool, or even a long-held assumption—was disrupted by something outside your control. How did you process that shift, and what did you do next?

Ugh, this one still stings a little. Early at FinansAnaliz, I’d spent *months* optimizing our PostgreSQL queries for the financial dashboards—like, hand-tuning every join, building materialized views, the whole nine yards. We’d just hit a 35% latency improvement, and I was feeling pretty smug.

Then the company signed a deal with a new data provider, and suddenly we had to ingest *real-time* market data from their Kafka streams. My beautiful, static queries? Useless. The CTO came to me and said, "Alara, we need this live in two weeks." Two weeks! I panicked—thought I’d have to scrap everything and start over.

But then I realized: the *logic* of my optimizations still mattered, just not the implementation. So I pivoted hard. Rewrote the core processing in Go for concurrency, slapped a Redis cache in front of it, and kept the PostgreSQL layer for historical data. It wasn’t perfect—still had to tweak the caching strategy for weeks—but we met the deadline, and the dashboards actually ended up *faster* than before because of the hybrid approach.

Biggest takeaway? Tech debt isn’t just about bad code—it’s about *assumptions*. I’d assumed our data would always be batch-processed, and that nearly sunk us. Now, I always ask, "What’s the *next* change coming?" before I get too attached to a solution.

Alara, this has been such an insightful conversation. You’ve shared some incredible examples of resilience, adaptability, and leadership—qualities we truly value here. I don’t have any more questions for you today, but I want to thank you for your time and for the thoughtful responses you’ve shared. 

We’ll be in touch soon with next steps, and I hope you have a wonderful rest of your day!
